- **Cleaning-in-Place (CIP) system** with caustic, acid, and water rinse tanks

**Working (2 marks):** Feed is pumped under low pressure into the module. The membrane acts as a physical barrier — components larger than the pore size are retained, while smaller molecules and water pass through. In **cross-flow mode**, the tangential feed velocity sweeps deposited particles off the membrane, maintaining flux.

**Applications in food industry (1 mark):**
1. **Cold pasteurisation of milk** (bacteria removal → ESL milk)
2. **Clarification of fruit juices** (apple, grape, citrus)
3. **Beer clarification** (yeast and protein haze removal)
4. **Wine clarification and cold stabilization**
5. **Sugar juice clarification** in sugar industry
6. **Process water treatment** in food plants

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $J = 250$ L·m⁻²·h⁻¹
- $A = 4$ m²
- $V_f = 2000$ L (feed)
- $V_p = 1600$ L (permeate)
- $C_f = 1.2\%$, $C_p = 0.05\%$ (pectin)

**(i) Volume of Retentate (2 marks)**

By mass balance: $V_f = V_p + V_r$

$$V_r = V_f - V_p = 2000 - 1600 = 400 \text{ L}$$

**(ii) Yield / Recovery (2 marks)**

$$Y = \frac{V_p}{V_f} = \frac{1600}{2000} = 0.80 \text{ or } 80\%$$

**(iii) Retention Coefficient (2 marks)**

$$R = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \frac{0.05}{1.2} = 1 - 0.0417 = 0.9583$$

This means the membrane retains **95.83% of pectin** — a very good clarification performance.

**(iv) Total Permeate Collected per Hour (1 mark)**

$$\text{Total permeate rate} = J \times A = 250 \times 4 = 1000 \text{ L/h}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern]**
*(a) What is membrane fouling? Explain the various fouling mechanisms in microfiltration and discuss the cleaning methods used in the food industry.* (7 marks)

*(b) A tubular ceramic microfiltration module is used to clarify apple juice. The membrane has a pure water permeability of **L_p = 380 L·m⁻²·h⁻¹·bar⁻¹**. After 4 hours of operation, the flux declines to **180 L·m⁻²·h⁻¹** at the same pressure of **1.2 bar**. Calculate: (i) The initial pure water flux, (ii) The fouling resistance ratio, (iii) The total resistance at the end of operation, and (iv) Comment on cleaning requirements.* (7 marks)

##### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Membrane Fouling: Mechanisms & Cleaning (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Membrane fouling is the **irreversible or partially reversible deposition of feed components** on the membrane surface or within its pores, leading to a decline in permeate flux and/or selectivity over time.

**Fouling Mechanisms (4 marks):**

1. **Cake Layer Formation**: Particles larger than the membrane pores accumulate on the surface, forming a porous cake. This is the dominant fouling mode in MF of fruit juices and milk.

2. **Pore Blocking** (4 sub-modes according to Hermia's model):
   - **Complete blocking**: Each particle seals one pore entirely.
   - **Standard blocking**: Particles deposit on pore walls, reducing pore diameter.
   - **Intermediate blocking**: Particles settle on previously deposited ones.
   - **Cake filtration**: Particles stack on top of each other.

3. **Adsorption Fouling**: Macromolecules (proteins, polyphenols) adsorb onto the membrane surface due to hydrophobic or electrostatic interactions.

4. **Biofouling**: Microbial growth on the membrane surface, forming a biofilm — critical in dairy and beverage applications.

**Cleaning Methods (2 marks):**
- **Physical Cleaning**: Backflushing, forward flushing, air sparging
- **Chemical Cleaning (CIP)**: Caustic (NaOH, 1–2%) for proteins; Acid (HNO₃, citric) for minerals; Enzymatic cleaners (proteases) for proteins; Chlorine-based disinfectants
- **Frequency**: Daily to weekly, depending on feed and flux decline

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $L_p = 380$ L·m⁻²·h⁻¹·bar⁻¹
- $J_{actual} = 180$ L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ after 4 h
- $\Delta P = 1.2$ bar
- Assume $\mu$ constant

**(i) Initial Pure Water Flux (2 marks)**

$$J_{water,initial} = L_p \times \Delta P = 380 \times 1.2 = 456 \text{ L·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Fouling Resistance Ratio (2 marks)**

From the resistance model:
$$\frac{J_{actual}}{J_{water}} = \frac{R_m}{R_t} = \frac{R_m}{R_m + R_f + R_c}$$

$$\frac{180}{456} = 0.3947$$

This means **only 39.47%** of the original flux remains — significant fouling has occurred.

**(iii) Total Resistance (2 marks)**

Using $J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_t}$, we need a reference. Assuming $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s and converting units:

$$R_t = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot J_{actual}}$$

Convert $\Delta P = 1.2 \text{ bar} = 1.2 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
Convert $J_{actual} = 180 \text{ L·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1} = \frac{180}{3600} \text{ L·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m·s}^{-1}$ (since 1 L = 10⁻³ m³)

Wait — let me redo in consistent SI:

$J_{actual} = 180 \times 10^{-3} / 3600 = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹

$$R_t = \frac{1.2 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^5}{5 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.4 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**(iv) Cleaning Comment (1 mark)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the fouling resistance ratio is only **0.3947** (i.e., flux has dropped by **60.5%**), a **CIP cycle must be initiated immediately**. Recommended: caustic wash (1% NaOH at 50°C) followed by acid rinse, then water flush until permeate is clear.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Mixing L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ with Pa·s in resistance calculations → unit errors. Always convert to **SI base units** before computing R.
> 2. **Confusing Retention vs Recovery**: Retention (R) is a *quality* parameter (separation efficiency). Recovery (Y) is a *quantity* parameter (volumetric yield). They are **NOT** the same.
> 3. **Forgetting the 1.0 cP → 10⁻³ Pa·s conversion** in milk/juice viscosity problems.
> 4. **Not labeling the Mermaid diagrams** clearly in Part B — examiners award marks for properly labeled schematics.
> 5. **Writing only formula without logic** in derivation questions — always narrate the *physical meaning* of each step.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2: Membrane Technologies & Microfiltration**

- [ ] **Membrane Technology** = physical separation using a selective barrier, **no phase change**, ambient temperature, low energy.
- [ ] The **four primary pressure-driven processes** are MF → UF → NF → RO, distinguished by **decreasing pore size** and **increasing operating pressure**.
- [ ] **MF pore size = 0.1 – 10 μm**; retains bacteria, yeast, suspended solids, fat globules.
- [ ] **Two operating modes**: Dead-end (simple, rapid fouling) and Cross-flow (industrial, steady-state).
- [ ] **Darcy's Law**: $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_t}$ — the foundation of all flux calculations.
- [ ] **Resistance in series**: $R_t = R_m + R_f + R_c$ — distinguishes membrane, fouling, and cake contributions.
- [ ] **Retention Coefficient**: $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$ → dimensionless, 0 to 1.
- [ ] **Recovery / Yield**: $Y = V_p/V_f$ → 0 to 1.
- [ ] **Fouling mechanisms**: cake formation, pore blocking (4 sub-modes per Hermia), adsorption, biofouling.
- [ ] **CIP cleaning**: caustic → acid → water rinse sequence.
- [ ] **Food industry applications**: milk (cold pasteurisation), juice (clarification), beer/wine (clarification), whey (protein concentration), sugar (juice clarification).
- [ ] **Industrial MF modules**: hollow fiber (dairy), ceramic monolith (beer/wine, harsh CIP), tubular (high viscosity, fouling feeds).
- [ ] **Pure water permeability** $L_p$ is a constant membrane property — measured with clean water before any fouling.
- [ ] Always use **SI units internally** when combining L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ with Pa·s (note: 1 L/m²/h = 1/3.6 × 10⁻⁶ m³/m²/s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Memory Triggers:**
> - **MF** → "Micro" = **0.1 – 10 μm** → "Bacteria" blocker
> - **UF** → "Ultra" = **1 – 100 nm** → "Protein" blocker
> - **NF** → "Nano" = **0.5 – 5 nm** → "Lactose + Divalent Salt" blocker
> - **RO** → "Reverse" = **< 0.5 nm non-porous** → "Water-only" permeation
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrafiltration (UF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ultrafiltration (UF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ultrafiltration (UF)** is a pressure-driven **membrane separation process** that employs semi-permeable membranes with nominal pore sizes in the range of **0.001 – 0.1 μm (1 – 100 nm)** to selectively separate macromolecules, colloids, suspended solids, and high-molecular-weight species from a feed solution, while allowing water, monovalent salts, and low-molecular-weight solutes to permeate through.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Ultrafiltration is classified as a **low-pressure membrane process** (typically operating between **1 – 10 bar**), fundamentally distinguished from microfiltration (larger pores, lower pressure) and nanofiltration/reverse osmosis (smaller pores, higher pressure) by its characteristic **molecular weight cut-off (MWCO)** in the range of **1,000 – 500,000 Daltons**.

The process is governed by a **size-exclusion (sieving) mechanism** rather than a thermodynamic phase-change mechanism, making it a purely physical separation technique that preserves the functional, nutritional, and sensory integrity of heat-sensitive food components.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Sieve" Intuition

Imagine you are making **filter coffee** at home using a traditional cloth filter:

- The **liquid coffee** (small molecules — water, caffeine, flavor volatiles) passes freely through the cloth.
- The **coffee grounds** (large particles) are retained on top.

Now scale this concept down to **molecular dimensions**:

| Everyday Coffee Sieve | Industrial Ultrafiltration Membrane |
|----------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Coffee grounds | Proteins, fat globules, microbes, colloids (RETAINED — **Retentate / Concentrate**) |
| Liquid coffee | Water, salts, sugars, vitamins (PERMEATE — **Permeate / Filtrate**) |
| Cloth filter pores | Membrane pores (0.001 – 0.1 μm) |
| Gravity + hand pressure | Applied pressure (1 – 10 bar) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Unlike boiling/evaporation (which uses heat and can damage flavors/nutrients), UF works **cold** — making it ideal for processing milk, fruit juices, and pharmaceuticals where heat-sensitive bioactive compounds must be retained.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in UF Characterisation

The following **bolded standard metrics** are universally applied to characterise ultrafiltration performance:

- **Permeate Flux (J)** — measured in **L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ (LMH)** or **m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹**
- **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP)** — measured in **bar** or **Pa**
- **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO)** — measured in **Daltons (Da)**
- **Rejection Coefficient (R)** — dimensionless (0 to 1)
- **Recovery Yield (Y)** — dimensionless percentage (0 – 100 %)

> [!TIP]
> For KTU numerical problems, always **retain units throughout your calculation** and convert to SI units (Pa for pressure, m³ for volume) only at the final step. Mark loss from unit inconsistency is the most common examiner comment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The UF process follows a well-defined sequence of mass-transfer events:

1. **Feed Introduction:** The feed liquid (e.g., skim milk, fruit juice, whey) is pumped into the membrane module under controlled pressure.
2. **Pressure Application:** A **transmembrane pressure (TMP)** is applied across the membrane. This is the **driving force** for permeation.
3. **Selective Sieving:** Components **larger than the membrane pore size** are physically retained on the upstream (retentate) side. Components **smaller than the pore size** pass through the membrane matrix.
4. **Retentate Recirculation:** The retained stream (concentrate) is either **recycled back to the feed tank** (batch / crossflow mode) or **discharged** (single-pass mode).
5. **Permeate Collection:** The filtrate (permeate) is collected separately for downstream processing or as the desired product.
6. **Fouling Onset:** Over time, retained solutes accumulate near the membrane surface, forming a **concentration-polarization layer** and eventually a **gel/cake layer**, reducing effective flux.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why UF works without heat:** The separation is purely **mechanical/size-based**. No phase change occurs. Energy input is only the pumping pressure. This is why UF is classified as a **non-thermal preservation** technique — critical for OEBBT Module-2 applications in dairy and juice processing.

## 2.2 Membrane Configurations in UF

UF membranes are fabricated in four principal geometric configurations, each suited to specific food applications:

| Configuration | Packing Density (m²/m³) | Typical Food Application | Fouling Resistance |
|---------------|------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|
| **Tubular** | Low (60–200) | High-viscosity fluids (tomato paste, starch) | Excellent (easy to clean) |
| **Hollow Fiber** | Very High (1,000–10,000) | Dairy whey, water treatment | Moderate |
| **Spiral Wound** | High (500–1,000) | Juice clarification, milk | Moderate |
| **Plate & Frame** | Low–Moderate (100–400) | Laboratory, high-value proteins | Good |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorisation Priority:** The following table consolidates **every standard UF equation** testable under KTU 2024 ESE Module-2. Each row is engineered for direct insertion into a 3-mark or 14-mark question solution.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variable Definitions | Standard Units |
|---|---------------|-------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Permeate Flux** | $J = \dfrac{V_p}{A_m \cdot t}$ | $V_p$ = permeate volume; $A_m$ = membrane area; $t$ = time | $\text{L·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}$ (LMH) |
| 2 | **Transmembrane Pressure** | $\Delta P_{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm}$ | $P_{in}$ = feed inlet pressure; $P_{out}$ = retentate outlet pressure; $P_{perm}$ = permeate pressure | Pa or bar |
| 3 | **Observed Rejection** | $R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | $C_p$ = permeate concentration; $C_f$ = feed concentration | Dimensionless |
| 4 | **Intrinsic Rejection** | $R_{int} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_{mw}}$ | $C_{mw}$ = concentration at membrane wall | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **Volumetric Recovery** | $Y = \dfrac{V_p}{V_f} \times 100\%$ | $V_p$ = permeate volume; $V_f$ = initial feed volume | % |
| 6 | **Resistance-in-Series (Darcy)** | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P_{TMP}}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$ | $\mu$ = viscosity; $R_{total}$ = total resistance | m·s⁻¹ |
| 7 | **Total Resistance** | $R_{total} = R_m + R_{cp} + R_g + R_f$ | $R_m$ = membrane; $R_{cp}$ = concentration polarization; $R_g$ = gel layer; $R_f$ = fouling | m⁻¹ |
| 8 | **Concentration Polarization** | $\dfrac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{J}{k}\right)$ | $C_w$ = wall concentration; $C_b$ = bulk concentration; $k$ = mass-transfer coefficient | Dimensionless |
| 9 | **Gel-Polarization Model** | $J_{lim} = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_g}{C_b}\right)$ | $C_g$ = gel concentration (empirical constant) | LMH |
| 10 | **Sieve / MWCO Relation** | $\text{MWCO} \approx \dfrac{\pi \cdot N_A \cdot d_p^3}{6} \cdot \rho$ | $d_p$ = pore diameter; $N_A$ = Avogadro; $\rho$ = solute density | Daltons (Da) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** In the rejection equations above, observe that $\dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ uses **fraction notation** (not the vertical pipe $\vert$), because $\vert C_p \vert$ would break the markdown table row. KTU valuation scripts may also fail if pipes appear inside equation cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of UF

Ultrafiltration has transformative utility across the food engineering sector:

- **Dairy Industry:** **Whey protein concentration** (recovering β-lactoglobulin and α-lactalbumin from cheese whey), milk standardization, and **cheese pre-concentration**.
- **Fruit Juice Processing:** **Cold clarification** of apple, grape, and pineapple juices — removing haze-forming pectins, starches, and proteins without heat-induced flavour loss.
- **Beverage Industry:** **Alcohol reduction** in wines and beers, and removal of off-flavours.
- **Edible Oils:** **Degumming and deacidification** of crude vegetable oils using membrane technology.
- **Enzyme Industry:** **Concentration and purification** of industrial enzymes (e.g., pectinase, amylase) from fermentation broths.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Permeate Flux from First Principles

**Problem Statement:** Derive the steady-state permeate flux equation for an ultrafiltration membrane operating under constant TMP, starting from the volumetric definition of flux.

**Given:** A membrane of area $A_m$ produces a permeate volume $V_p$ in time $t$ under a transmembrane pressure $\Delta P_{TMP}$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$.

**Derivation:**

The volumetric flux $J$ is **defined** as the permeate volume flowing through unit membrane area per unit time:

$$J \;=\; \lim_{\Delta t \to 0}\,\dfrac{\Delta V_p}{A_m \,\cdot\, \Delta t}$$

For **steady-state operation** (which is the design assumption in KTU problems), the flux becomes constant, allowing us to drop the limit operator and write:

$$J \;=\; \dfrac{V_p}{A_m \cdot t}$$

**Step 2 — Dimensional check:**
The dimension of $J$ is:

$$[J] \;=\; \dfrac{[\text{L}]}{[\text{L}^2] \cdot [\text{T}]} \;=\; \text{L·T}^{-1}$$

This is consistent with the standard SI unit **m·s⁻¹**, which is equivalent to the industry-preferred **LMH** (L·m⁻²·h⁻¹) after the conversion factor **1 m·s⁻¹ = 3.6 × 10⁶ LMH**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resistance-in-Series Model

**Problem Statement:** Show that the total hydraulic resistance opposing permeate flow can be decomposed additively into four contributing resistances.

**Derivation:**

Start from **Darcy's Law** for flow through a porous medium:

$$J \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P_{TMP}}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$$

**Step 1 — Membrane resistance.** For a **clean** membrane, the resistance is purely structural:

$$R_m \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P_{TMP}}{\mu \cdot J_0}$$

where $J_0$ is the **pure-water flux** measured experimentally with deionized water.

**Step 2 — Add concentration polarization resistance $R_{cp}$.** As solute accumulates at the membrane surface, an additional osmotic and viscous resistance arises:

$$R_{cp} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta \pi}{\mu \cdot J}$$

where $\Delta \pi$ is the osmotic pressure difference between the wall and permeate.

**Step 3 — Add gel layer resistance $R_g$.** When wall concentration reaches the gel concentration $C_g$, a quasi-solid layer forms:

$$R_g \;=\; \dfrac{\text{(gel thickness)} \cdot \mu}{\text{(gel permeability)}}$$

**Step 4 — Add fouling resistance $R_f$.** Irreversible adsorption, pore-blocking, and biofilm accumulation contribute a permanent resistance:

$$R_f \;=\; R_{f,\text{ads}} + R_{f,\text{pore}} + R_{f,\text{bio}}$$

**Step 5 — Combine linearly (assumption of independent additive resistances):**

$$R_{total} \;=\; R_m + R_{cp} + R_g + R_f$$

Substituting back into Darcy's Law:

$$J \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P_{TMP}}{\mu \,(R_m + R_{cp} + R_g + R_f)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU valuation:** This derivation is worth **full 7 marks** in a 14-mark Part-B question if the student explicitly lists each resistance and explains its physical origin. Examiners report that students typically lose 2–3 marks by omitting $R_g$ (gel layer) or by failing to define $\mu$ (viscosity) and its units.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Concentration Polarization (Film Theory)

**Problem Statement:** Derive the steady-state film-theory equation relating wall concentration $C_w$ to bulk concentration $C_b$ for a membrane under flux $J$.

**Derivation:**

Consider a **differential control volume** of thickness $dx$ adjacent to the membrane surface. At steady state, the **convective flux toward the membrane** equals the **diffusive flux back into the bulk**:

**Convective transport toward membrane (per unit area):**

$$J \cdot C(x)$$

**Diffusive back-transport (Fick's Law):**

$$-D \,\dfrac{dC(x)}{dx}$$

**Setting convective = diffusive at steady state:**

$$J \cdot C(x) \;=\; -D \,\dfrac{dC(x)}{dx}$$

**Rearranging:**

$$\dfrac{dC}{C} \;=\; -\dfrac{J}{D}\,dx$$

**Integrating from the bulk ($x = \delta$, $C = C_b$) to the wall ($x = 0$, $C = C_w$):**

$$\int_{C_b}^{C_w} \dfrac{dC}{C} \;=\; -\dfrac{J}{D}\int_{\delta}^{0} dx$$

**Evaluating:**

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_w}{C_b}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{J \cdot \delta}{D}$$

Recognising the **mass-transfer coefficient** $k = D/\delta$:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p} \;=\; \exp\!\left(\dfrac{J}{k}\right)}$$

For a **fully rejected solute** ($R = 1 \Rightarrow C_p = 0$), this simplifies to:

$$C_w \;=\; C_b \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{J}{k}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Insight:** When $J \to 0$, $C_w \to C_b$ (no polarization). When $J$ is large, $C_w \to C_g$ (gel-limited flux). This is the conceptual basis of the **gel-polarization model** $J_{lim} = k \ln(C_g / C_b)$.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** A UF module with **2.5 m²** active membrane area processes **50 L** of whey feed. After **2 hours** of operation, the permeate collected measures **38 L**, and the inlet/outlet pressures are **4.2 bar** and **3.6 bar**, respectively. The permeate side is at atmospheric pressure. Calculate (a) the average flux in LMH, (b) the TMP, and (c) the volumetric recovery.

**Solution:**

**(a) Permeate Flux:**

$$J \;=\; \dfrac{V_p}{A_m \cdot t} \;=\; \dfrac{38\,\text{L}}{2.5\,\text{m}^2 \times 2\,\text{h}} \;=\; 7.6\,\text{LMH}$$

**(b) TMP (converting bar to Pa is unnecessary for ratio):**

$$\Delta P_{TMP} \;=\; \dfrac{4.2 + 3.6}{2} - 1.013 \;=\; 3.9 - 1.013 \;=\; 2.887\,\text{bar}$$

**(c) Recovery:**

$$Y \;=\; \dfrac{V_p}{V_f} \times 100\% \;=\; \dfrac{38}{50} \times 100\% \;=\; 76\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a UF System

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **process flow architecture** of a complete crossflow UF plant, including all standard auxiliary subsystems (feed tank, pump, pressure gauges, retentate recycle, permeate collection, and CIP — Clean-in-Place).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedTank[FT01 Feed Tank] --> feedPump[P01 Feed Pump]
    feedPump --> inletGauge[PI01 Inlet Pressure Gauge]
    inletGauge --> membraneMod[M01 UF Membrane Module]
    membraneMod --> outletGauge[PI02 Outlet Pressure Gauge]
    outletGauge --> throtVal[V01 Throttle Valve]
    throtVal --> recycleLine[Recycle Line Back to Feed Tank]
    membraneMod --> permGauge[PI03 Permeate Pressure Gauge]
    permGauge --> permCollect[T01 Permeate Collection Tank]
    feedTank --> tempCtrl[H01 Heat Exchanger Temperature Control]
    tempCtrl --> feedPump
    recycleLine --> feedTank
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows indicate the **direction of bulk mass flow**. The pressure gauges PI01, PI02, and PI03 are positioned at the three points required by the TMP formula: inlet, outlet (retentate side), and permeate side. The throttle valve V01 is used to **adjust the cross-flow velocity** and hence the TMP — increasing V01 closure raises the system pressure, increasing TMP.

## 4.2 Conceptual Cross-Section of a UF Membrane — Functional Block View

The following diagram represents a **conceptual cross-section** of the membrane separation zone, mapping the four mass-transfer phenomena occurring simultaneously at the membrane interface.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph upstream[Upstream Retentate Side]
        bulkFeed[Bulk Feed Solution with Concentration Cb]
        boundaryLayer[Concentration Polarization Layer with Concentration increasing Cb to Cw]
        gelLayer[Gel Layer with Critical Concentration Cg]
    end
    subgraph membrane[UF Membrane Matrix]
        membranePore[Asymmetric Membrane with Pore Size 0.001 to 0.1 micrometer]
        membraneBody[Support Layer providing Mechanical Strength]
    end
    subgraph downstream[Downstream Permeate Side]
        permeateStream[Purified Permeate with Concentration Cp]
        fluxArrow[Permeate Flux J flowing perpendicular to Membrane]
    end
    bulkFeed --> boundaryLayer
    boundaryLayer --> gelLayer
    gelLayer --> membranePore
    membranePore --> permeateStream
    membraneBody --> membranePore
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The progression $C_b \to C_w \to C_g$ represents the **concentration build-up** as one approaches the membrane surface. The membrane itself is **asymmetric** — a thin, dense skin (top) provides selectivity, while a thicker porous sub-layer provides mechanical strength. This is the standard structure of all commercial UF membranes (e.g., polyethersulfone, regenerated cellulose).

## 4.3 Decision Tree for UF Module Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ{What is the Feed Viscosity and Fouling Potential?}
    startQ -->|Low viscosity clean fluid| hollowFiber[Choose Hollow Fiber UF Module]
    startQ -->|High viscosity fouling prone fluid| tubularMod[Choose Tubular UF Module]
    startQ -->|Moderate viscosity dairy fluid| spiralWound[Choose Spiral Wound UF Module]
    startQ -->|High value lab scale protein| plateFrame[Choose Plate and Frame UF Module]
    hollowFiber --> dairyApp[Application: Whey Protein Concentration and Water Reuse]
    tubularMod --> juiceApp[Application: Tomato Paste Clarification and Starch Processing]
    spiralWound --> milkApp[Application: Milk Standardization and Cheese Pre-concentration]
    plateFrame --> pharmaApp[Application: Laboratory Enzyme Purification]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 3-mark question asks: *"Justify the selection of a tubular UF module for tomato juice clarification."* The correct answer must reference **(a)** the high viscosity of tomato concentrate, **(b)** the suspended solids loading, and **(c)** the ease of mechanical cleaning of tubular geometries — all three reasons must be stated.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Ultrafiltration. State any two applications of UF in the food processing industry. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks — approximately 80–100 words):**

Ultrafiltration (UF) is a **pressure-driven membrane separation process** employing semi-permeable membranes with pore sizes in the range **0.001 to 0.1 μm** to selectively retain macromolecules such as proteins, colloids, and microorganisms while allowing water, salts, and small solutes to permeate through. It typically operates at low transmembrane pressures of **1 to 10 bar**.

**Two applications:**
1. **Whey protein concentration** in the dairy industry — recovering valuable β-lactoglobulin from cheese whey for use in infant formula and sports nutrition.
2. **Cold clarification of fruit juices** — removing haze-forming pectins, starches, and suspended pulp from apple, grape, and pineapple juices without heat-induced flavour loss.

### Question 2: Differentiate between Microfiltration (MF) and Ultrafiltration (UF) with respect to pore size, operating pressure, and typical retained species. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks — tabulated for clarity):**

| Parameter | Microfiltration (MF) | Ultrafiltration (UF) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Pore size range** | 0.1 – 10 μm | 0.001 – 0.1 μm |
| **Operating pressure** | 0.1 – 2 bar | 1 – 10 bar |
| **Typical retained species** | Bacteria, yeast, suspended solids | Proteins, macromolecules, viruses, colloids |
| **MWCO** | Not typically defined | 1,000 – 500,000 Da |
| **Example food application** | Beer clarification, milk fat removal | Whey concentration, juice clarification |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive UF Analysis `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**Question A(a)** — **[7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of a crossflow ultrafiltration system. Describe the role of transmembrane pressure and cross-flow velocity in maintaining sustainable flux.

**Model Solution A(a):**

**Working Principle of Crossflow UF:**

In crossflow (also called tangential flow) UF, the feed stream flows **parallel** to the membrane surface while a fraction of the liquid permeates **perpendicularly** through the membrane. This arrangement is fundamentally different from **dead-end filtration**, where all flow is perpendicular.

**Key Components of the Crossflow UF System:**

1. **Feed Tank:** Holds the feed liquid (e.g., whey, juice). Often equipped with a stirrer and temperature control.
2. **Feed Pump:** A centrifugal or positive-displacement pump pressurises the feed to the desired TMP.
3. **Membrane Module:** Contains the UF membrane in one of the standard configurations (tubular, hollow fibre, spiral wound, plate and frame).
4. **Pressure Indicators:** Two gauges — one at the inlet, one at the outlet of the membrane module — used to calculate the average TMP.
5. **Throttle Valve:** Located on the retentate line, it controls the outlet pressure and hence the TMP.
6. **Retentate Recycle Line:** Returns unpermeated concentrate to the feed tank for further processing.
7. **Permeate Collection Vessel:** Collects the filtered product for downstream use.

**Role of Transmembrane Pressure (TMP):**

The TMP is the **net driving force** for permeation. As TMP increases, flux increases linearly (at low values) according to Darcy's Law. However, beyond a critical pressure, the flux becomes **independent of TMP** due to **concentration polarization** and **gel-layer formation**.

**Role of Cross-Flow Velocity (CFV):**

High cross-flow velocity increases **shear stress** at the membrane surface, which **sweeps away accumulated solute** and reduces the thickness of the concentration polarization layer. This allows higher sustainable flux. However, excessive CFV increases pumping cost and may damage shear-sensitive food components (e.g., proteins, emulsions).

**Diagram:** (See SECTION 4.1 above for the complete Mermaid block-level diagram of this system.)

---

**Question A(b)** — **[7 Marks]** A dairy plant operates a UF system to concentrate whey proteins. The system has a **membrane area of 10 m²**, the feed contains **4.0 % (w/w) protein**, the permeate contains **0.05 % (w/w) protein**, the feed flow is **200 L/h**, and the permeate flow is **120 L/h**. Calculate: (i) the observed rejection coefficient, (ii) the permeate flux in LMH, and (iii) the volumetric concentration factor.

**Model Solution A(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Membrane area $A_m = 10\,\text{m}^2$
- Feed protein concentration $C_f = 4.0\,\%$
- Permeate protein concentration $C_p = 0.05\,\%$
- Feed flow $Q_f = 200\,\text{L/h}$
- Permeate flow $Q_p = 120\,\text{L/h}$

**(i) Observed Rejection Coefficient:**

$$R_{obs} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{0.05}{4.0} \;=\; 1 - 0.0125 \;=\; 0.9875$$

Hence, the UF membrane rejects **98.75 %** of the protein — a typical high-performance value for dairy UF.

**(ii) Permeate Flux in LMH:**

$$J \;=\; \dfrac{Q_p}{A_m} \;=\; \dfrac{120\,\text{L/h}}{10\,\text{m}^2} \;=\; 12\,\text{LMH}$$

**(iii) Volumetric Concentration Factor (VCF):**

The retentate flow is $Q_r = Q_f - Q_p = 200 - 120 = 80\,\text{L/h}$.

$$\text{VCF} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_f}{Q_r} \;=\; \dfrac{200}{80} \;=\; 2.5$$

Hence, the whey is concentrated **2.5-fold** in volume — equivalent to an effective protein concentration of approximately **10 % (w/w)** in the retentate (assuming complete mass balance of protein).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Membrane Fouling and Process Design `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

**Question B(a)** — **[7 Marks]** Discuss the phenomenon of **concentration polarization** and **membrane fouling** in ultrafiltration. Explain the **gel-polarization model** and state how it is used to predict the **limiting flux** in UF operation.

**Model Solution B(a):**

**Concentration Polarization (CP):**

Concentration polarization is the **reversible accumulation** of retained solute at the membrane surface due to the convective flow of solvent toward the membrane exceeding the back-diffusive transport of solute into the bulk. This produces a thin layer (typically 10–100 μm thick) where the solute concentration rises from $C_b$ in the bulk to $C_w$ at the wall.

CP is **reversible** — it can be reduced by increasing cross-flow velocity, by using turbulence promoters, or by periodic backflushing.

**Membrane Fouling:**

Fouling is the **irreversible** deposition and adsorption of solutes within or on the membrane, including:
- **Pore blocking** — partial or complete obstruction of membrane pores.
- **Cake/gel formation** — build-up of a compressible solid layer on the membrane surface.
- **Adsorptive fouling** — chemical interaction of solutes with the membrane polymer.
- **Biofouling** — microbial colonisation and biofilm formation.

Fouling cannot be removed by hydrodynamic means alone; **chemical cleaning** (CIP with NaOH, acids, or enzymatic detergents) is required.

**Gel-Polarization Model:**

When the wall concentration $C_w$ reaches a critical **gel concentration** $C_g$, the flux becomes independent of TMP. Under this assumption, the steady-state flux is:

$$J_{lim} \;=\; k \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_g}{C_b}\right)$$

where:
- $J_{lim}$ = **limiting flux** (LMH)
- $k = D/\delta$ = mass-transfer coefficient (m·s⁻¹)
- $C_g$ = gel concentration (empirical; depends on solute type, e.g., ~20–30 % for whey proteins)
- $C_b$ = bulk concentration (kg·m⁻³)

**Application to Flux Prediction:**

- The model predicts that flux increases **logarithmically** as bulk concentration decreases.
- Operating above the limiting flux wastes pumping energy without additional throughput.
- The model guides the selection of optimal $C_b$ (e.g., when concentrating whey, avoid exceeding $C_b$ values that push $J_{lim}$ below the design flux).

---

**Question B(b)** — **[7 Marks]** Compare **tubular, hollow-fibre, and spiral-wound** UF membrane configurations in terms of packing density, suitability for high-viscosity feeds, ease of cleaning, and typical food industry application. Justify the selection of a tubular module for tomato juice clarification.

**Model Solution B(b):**

| Criterion | Tubular | Hollow Fibre | Spiral Wound |
|-----------|---------|--------------|--------------|
| **Packing Density (m²/m³)** | 60 – 200 | 1,000 – 10,000 | 500 – 1,000 |
| **Suitability for High-Viscosity Feeds** | Excellent | Poor (channel blockage) | Moderate |
| **Ease of Cleaning** | Excellent (mechanical sponge) | Moderate (backflush) | Moderate (chemical only) |
| **Capital Cost per m²** | High | Low | Moderate |
| **Typical Food Application** | Tomato paste, starch | Whey, water reuse | Milk, juice |

**Justification for Tomato Juice Clarification (Tubular Module):**

1. **High viscosity:** Tomato juice and concentrate have viscosities in the range **50–500 mPa·s**, far exceeding the tolerance of hollow-fibre and spiral-wound channels. Tubular channels (typically 12–25 mm diameter) accommodate viscous flow without excessive pressure drop.
2. **Suspended solids loading:** Tomato pulp and cell-wall fragments cause rapid fouling. Tubular membranes allow **mechanical sponge-ball cleaning** in addition to CIP, ensuring sustainable long-term operation.
3. **Shear tolerance:** The wide tubular geometry withstands high cross-flow velocities required for shear-thinning of tomato concentrate.
4. **Operational reliability:** Plant records from commercial tomato processing lines show tubular UF achieves **>90 % recovery** of clarified serum with **<5 % flux decline per hour** — a performance unmatched by spiral-wound or hollow-fibre systems in this duty.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Defining UF without specifying pore size or pressure range** → Lose 1 mark. Always quantify the definition.
> 2. **Confusing MWCO with pore diameter** → Lose 1 mark. MWCO is in Daltons (a molecular-weight property), pore diameter is in μm (a geometric property). They are *related* by Equation 10, but not interchangeable.
> 3. **Omitting units in numerical answers** → Lose 1 mark. Even a correct number without "LMH", "bar", or "%" is incomplete.
> 4. **Writing $J = V/A$ without the time variable** → Lose 0.5 mark. Flux is fundamentally **volumetric throughput per unit area per unit time**.
> 5. **Confusing $R_{obs}$ with $R_{int}$** → Lose 1 mark. $R_{obs}$ uses the **bulk feed** concentration, $R_{int}$ uses the **membrane-wall** concentration.
> 6. **Stating "tubular is best" without justifying all three reasons** (viscosity, solids, cleanability) → Lose 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Before Exam — Memorise These Bullets:**

- **UF Definition:** Pressure-driven membrane process, pore size **0.001 – 0.1 μm**, MWCO **1,000 – 500,000 Da**, operating pressure **1 – 10 bar**.
- **Primary Driving Force:** **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP)**, NOT temperature.
- **Separation Mechanism:** **Size exclusion (sieving)** — purely physical, no phase change.
- **Two Process Modes:** **Dead-end** (batch, lower capital, higher fouling) and **Crossflow** (continuous, higher flux sustainability).
- **Flux Equation (must memorise):** $J = \dfrac{V_p}{A_m \cdot t}$ — units **LMH**.
- **TMP Equation (must memorise):** $\Delta P_{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm}$.
- **Rejection Equation (must memorise):** $R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$.
- **Resistance-in-Series Model:** $R_{total} = R_m + R_{cp} + R_g + R_f$ — each term represents a distinct physical phenomenon.
- **Gel-Polarization Limiting Flux:** $J_{lim} = k \ln(C_g / C_b)$ — explains why flux plateaus at high TMP.
- **Concentration Polarization Equation:** $\dfrac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp(J/k)$.
- **Module Selection Rule:** **Tubular → high viscosity / high solids; Hollow fibre → low viscosity / clean fluid; Spiral wound → dairy; Plate-and-frame → lab scale.**
- **Dairy Application:** Whey protein concentration, milk standardization, cheese pre-concentration.
- **Juice Application:** Cold clarification of apple, grape, pineapple juices.
- **UF is NON-THERMAL** — preserves heat-sensitive vitamins, flavours, and bioactive compounds.
- **Fouling is irreversible; CP is reversible** — distinguish these clearly in long-answer questions.
- **Always carry units** in numerical answers; convert only at the final step.
- **Typical industrial flux values:** 5 – 50 LMH (much lower than pure water flux due to fouling).
- **Typical industrial TMP:** 1 – 5 bar for food-grade UF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End-of-Notes Marker:** All topics listed above are **directly aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Module-2 (OEBBT611 — Fundamentals of Food Processing)** learning outcomes **CO2** and **CO3**, which require students to *describe* and *apply* the principles of membrane-based separation processes in food engineering contexts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Nanofiltration (NF), Reverse Osmosis (RO), and Their Industrial Applications; Pulsed Electric Field Processing: Principle

---

# Membrane Separation Technologies: NF & RO + Pulsed Electric Field Processing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Nanofiltration (NF) — The "Smart Sieve"

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Nanofiltration (NF) is a pressure-driven membrane separation process that operates between Ultrafiltration (UF) and Reverse Osmosis (RO), with nominal pore sizes ranging from **0.5 nm to 2 nm** and a Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO) typically between **200 Da and 1000 Da**. It preferentially rejects multivalent ions and larger organic molecules while allowing monovalent ions (e.g., Na⁺, Cl⁻) to pass partially.*

**Operational Window:**
- **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP):** **0.5 MPa – 2 MPa** (5 – 20 bar)
- **Operating Temperature:** **15 °C – 45 °C**
- **Membrane Type:** Thin-Film Composite (TFC), typically polyamide on polysulfone support.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Bouncer at the Club":**
> Imagine a nightclub bouncer who lets in anyone wearing sneakers (small monovalent salts like NaCl), but strictly rejects anyone in heavy boots or carrying large backpacks (multivalent salts like CaSO₄, lactose, dyes, proteins). NF behaves exactly like this selective bouncer — its membrane carries a slight **negative surface charge** that repels divalent and trivalent ions (electrostatic repulsion + size exclusion) while letting small univalent ions slide through.

---

### 1.2 Reverse Osmosis (RO) — The "Absolute Filter"

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Reverse Osmosis is a pressure-driven membrane separation process in which an applied hydraulic pressure greater than the **osmotic pressure** of the feed solution forces the solvent (typically water) through a **dense, non-porous (or near-porous 0.1–1 nm)** semipermeable membrane, while rejecting dissolved solutes, ions, and macromolecules almost completely.*

**Operational Window:**
- **Applied Pressure:** **1 MPa – 10 MPa** (10 – 100 bar); brackish water ~10–25 bar, seawater ~55–80 bar.
- **Rejection (R) for NaCl:** **95 % – 99.8 %**
- **Membrane Material:** Cellulose acetate (older); Polyamide Thin-Film Composite (modern, dominant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Pushing Water Uphill Against Nature":**
> Osmosis naturally pulls water *toward* the salty side (water flows from low → high solute concentration). In RO, we forcibly push water *away* from the salty side by applying pressure higher than the osmotic pressure. Picture a crowded room (high solute side); the people (water) naturally push to enter. RO is like the security guard pushing them back out the door with enough force to overcome their eagerness to squeeze in.

---

### 1.3 Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) Processing — The "Cell Membrane Zipper"

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) processing is a non-thermal food preservation technique in which short-duration high-voltage pulses (**µs to ms**) are applied across food placed between two electrodes, generating electric field strengths typically of **20 kV/cm – 40 kV/cm**. The pulses induce **electroporation** of microbial and plant cell membranes, leading to loss of membrane integrity and inactivation of microorganisms.*

**Key Pulse Parameters:**
- **Electric Field Strength (E):** **20 – 40 kV/cm**
- **Pulse Duration (tp):** **1 µs – 100 µs**
- **Number of Pulses (n):** **5 – 50**
- **Total Treatment Time:** **< 1 second** (preserves sensory/nutritional quality)
- **Energy Input:** **~ 100 – 400 kJ/kg**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Lightning Striking a Balloon":**
> Picture a row of water balloons (microbial cells). When a strong, brief electric pulse strikes them, the thin rubber skin (cell membrane) develops tiny permanent pores — like poking a balloon with a needle — and the contents leak out, killing the cell. Critically, larger, sturdier food-tissue cells can recover (reversible electroporation) while microbes cannot (irreversible electroporation). This *selectivity* is what makes PEF so valuable.

---

### 1.4 Membrane Separation Spectrum (Positioning NF & RO)

> [!TIP]
> Always remember this hierarchy when answering KTU exam questions on membrane processes:

| Process | Pore Size | MWCO | Typical ΔP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Microfiltration (MF) | 0.1 – 10 µm | > 1000 kDa | 0.1 – 0.5 bar |
| Ultrafiltration (UF) | 1 – 100 nm | 1 – 500 kDa | 0.5 – 5 bar |
| **Nanofiltration (NF)** | **0.5 – 2 nm** | **200 – 1000 Da** | **5 – 20 bar** |
| **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | **< 0.5 nm (dense)** | **< 200 Da** | **10 – 80 bar** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Solution-Diffusion Model (Governing RO & NF Transport Theory)

The **Solution-Diffusion (S-D) model** (Lonsdale, 1965) is the *gold standard* model used in KTU textbook treatments of RO/NF. It assumes the membrane is a **dense, non-porous polymer** through which both solvent and solute dissolve and then diffuse down chemical potential gradients.

**Step-by-step logic:**

- **Step 1:** Solvent (water) and solute dissolve *into* the membrane's upstream face (governed by partition coefficient *K*).
- **Step 2:** Both species diffuse through the membrane matrix under a chemical potential gradient.
- **Step 3:** Solute and solvent desorb on the permeate side.
- **Why this matters:** Rejection in NF/RO is *not* purely a "sieving" effect — it is dominated by the **solubility** and **diffusivity** of the solute *inside* the polymer.

---

### 2.2 Reverse Osmosis — Hydraulic and Osmotic Pressure Balance

**Mass-Transfer Derivation (Critical for 7-Mark Problems):**

For RO, the **net driving pressure** is:

$$\Delta P_{\text{net}} = \Delta P - \Delta \pi$$

Where:
- $\Delta P = P_{\text{feed}} - P_{\text{permeate}}$ (hydraulic pressure difference)
- $\Delta \pi = \pi_{\text{feed}} - \pi_{\text{permeate}}$ (osmotic pressure difference)
- **Threshold condition:** RO requires $\Delta P > \Delta \pi$, otherwise no net flux occurs.

**The Van't Hoff Equation** for ideal dilute solutions:

$$\pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$$

Where:
- $i$ = Van't Hoff factor (number of dissociated ions; NaCl → 2, CaCl₂ → 3)
- $C$ = molar concentration (mol/m³)
- $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** (Universal Gas Constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Solvent (Water) Flux Equation:**

$$J_w = A_w \left( \Delta P - \Delta \pi \right)$$

Where $A_w$ is the **water permeability constant** (m³/(m²·s·Pa)).

**Solute Flux Equation:**

$$J_s = B_s \left( C_f - C_p \right)$$

Where $B_s$ is the **solute permeability constant** (m/s), and $C_f, C_p$ are feed and permeate solute concentrations.

**Observed Rejection (R):**

$$R = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** The S-D model is used to design spiral-wound RO modules for seawater desalination plants (e.g., NEOM, Jeddah SWRO plants producing 1 Mm³/day) and brackish water reclamation systems in Kerala (KWA brackish groundwater treatment).

---

### 2.3 Nanofiltration — Hybrid Sieving + Charge Mechanism

NF transport combines:
1. **Steric (size) hindrance** → larger species blocked.
2. **Donnan exclusion** → membrane surface charge repels co-ions (ions of same charge as membrane).
3. **Solution-Diffusion** (residual contribution).

The **Spiegler-Kedem equation** (advanced; sometimes used in KTU Module 2 derivations):

$$J_v = L_p \left( \Delta P - \sigma \cdot \Delta \pi \right)$$

$$J_s = P_s \left( C_f - C_p \right) + \left( 1 - \sigma \right) \cdot C_{\text{avg}} \cdot J_v$$

Where:
- $L_p$ = hydraulic permeability (m/s·Pa)
- $\sigma$ = **reflection coefficient** ($\sigma \rightarrow 1$ for complete rejection; $\sigma \rightarrow 0$ for free passage)
- $P_s$ = solute permeability (m/s)
- $C_{\text{avg}}$ = average solute concentration across membrane

---

### 2.4 Pulsed Electric Field — Critical Field Strength & Electroporation

When a microbial cell (radius $r$) is exposed to an external electric field $E$, the induced **transmembrane potential** $V_m$ is:

$$V_m = 0.75 \cdot r \cdot E \cdot \cos\theta$$

Where $\theta$ is the angle between the field direction and the site on the membrane. The critical breakdown voltage for biological membranes is approximately **$V_c \approx 1$ V**.

The **critical electric field** required for electroporation:

$$E_c = \frac{V_c}{0.75 \cdot r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a typical bacterium with $r \approx 0.5 \, \mu m$, $E_c \approx 2.7 \, kV/cm$. Commercial PEF systems therefore use **20 – 40 kV/cm** to ensure robust inactivation well above $E_c$.

**Pulse energy density** delivered to the food:

$$W = n \cdot E^2 \cdot \tau \cdot \sigma_{\text{elec}}$$

Where $n$ = number of pulses, $\tau$ = pulse duration, $\sigma_{\text{elec}}$ = electrical conductivity of the food.

---

### 2.5 📘 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is **exhaustive** for KTU Part B questions on this topic. Memorize every entry.

| # | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J_w = A_w (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | $J_w$ [m/s], $A_w$ [m/(s·Pa)] | RO water flux |
| 2 | $J_s = B_s (C_f - C_p)$ | $J_s$ [mol/(m²·s)] | RO solute flux |
| 3 | $\pi = iCRT$ | $i$ [-], $C$ [mol/m³], $R$ [J/(mol·K)], $T$ [K] | Osmotic pressure (ideal) |
| 4 | $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$ | dimensionless [-] | Observed rejection |
| 5 | $J_v = L_p (\Delta P - \sigma \Delta \pi)$ | $L_p$ [m/(s·Pa)] | NF volumetric flux |
| 6 | $V_m = 0.75 \cdot r \cdot E \cos\theta$ | $V_m$ [V], $r$ [m], $E$ [V/m] | PEF induced TMP |
| 7 | $E_c = V_c / (0.75 \cdot r)$ | $E_c$ [V/m] | PEF critical field |
| 8 | $W = n \cdot E^2 \cdot \tau \cdot \sigma_{\text{elec}}$ | $W$ [J/m³] | PEF energy density |
| 9 | $\Delta \pi = RT \cdot \Delta C_s$ (for NaCl, $i=2$) | [Pa] | Osmotic ΔP |
| 10 | $\text{Recovery} = Q_p / Q_f \times 100\%$ | [%] | System efficiency |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NF** → *Dairy industry*: **Lactose concentration** (whey demineralization), partial desalination of cheese brine, color removal in sugar refining.
- **RO** → *Seawater desalination*, fruit juice **concentration** (apple, orange), milk concentration for **cheese yield improvement**, wastewater reuse in food plants.
- **PEF** → *Juice pasteurization* (preserves vitamin C & fresh flavor), **potato** & apple pretreatment for *enhanced drying*, improvement of *mass transfer* during diffusion-extraction processes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Osmotic Pressure Using Van't Hoff Equation

> **Problem:** Calculate the osmotic pressure of a **0.5 mol/L NaCl solution at 25 °C**.

**Step 1 — Convert to SI units:**

$$C = 0.5 \; \text{mol/L} = 0.5 \times 10^3 \; \text{mol/m}^3 = 500 \; \text{mol/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Determine Van't Hoff factor $i$:**

NaCl dissociates fully in dilute solution:

$$\text{NaCl} \rightarrow \text{Na}^+ + \text{Cl}^-$$

Therefore:

$$i = 2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Van't Hoff equation:**

$$\pi = i \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$$

$$\pi = 2 \times 500 \times 8.314 \times (25 + 273)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate temperature:**

$$T = 25 + 273 = 298 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 5 — Compute:**

$$\pi = 2 \times 500 \times 8.314 \times 298$$

$$\pi = 1000 \times 2477.572$$

$$\pi \approx 2.477 \times 10^6 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to bar for engineering intuition:**

$$\pi \approx 24.77 \; \text{bar}$$

> [!TIP]
> This 24.77 bar explains why **seawater RO** must apply **≥ 55 bar** — the osmotic pressure is the *floor* of the applied pressure.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Membrane Rejection for a Single-Stage RO System

> **Problem:** An RO system processes feed with $C_f = 35{,}000 \; \text{ppm}$ NaCl. The permeate has $C_p = 350 \; \text{ppm}$. Find (a) Rejection $R$, (b) the *real* hydraulic requirement if $\Delta \pi$ from feed to permeate concentration must be overcome.

**Part (a) — Observed Rejection:**

$$R = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f}$$

$$R = 1 - \frac{350}{35{,}000}$$

$$R = 1 - 0.01$$

$$R = 0.99 \; \text{or} \; 99\%$$

**Part (b) — Required Hydraulic Pressure:**

First, compute feed-side osmotic pressure using Van't Hoff:
- $C_f = 35{,}000 \; \text{ppm} \approx 35 \; \text{g/L}$
- Molar mass NaCl = **58.44 g/mol**
- $C_f = 35 / 58.44 \approx 0.599 \; \text{mol/L} = 599 \; \text{mol/m}^3$

$$\pi_f = 2 \times 599 \times 8.314 \times 298$$

$$\pi_f \approx 2.97 \times 10^6 \; \text{Pa} \approx 29.7 \; \text{bar}$$

For permeate ($\approx 350$ ppm → $C_p \approx 6 \; \text{mol/m}^3$):

$$\pi_p = 2 \times 6 \times 8.314 \times 298 \approx 0.030 \; \text{bar}$$

$$\Delta \pi = \pi_f - \pi_p \approx 29.7 \; \text{bar}$$

For non-zero flux:

$$P_{\text{applied}} > \Delta \pi \approx 29.7 \; \text{bar}$$

In practice, **55–80 bar** is applied to overcome concentration polarization and fouling layers.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Critical Field Strength for *E. coli* Inactivation

> **Problem:** *E. coli* has a typical radius $r = 0.5 \; \mu m$. The critical membrane breakdown potential is $V_c = 1 \; \text{V}$. Calculate the critical electric field strength.

**Step 1 — Convert radius to meters:**

$$r = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply critical field equation:**

$$E_c = \frac{V_c}{0.75 \cdot r}$$

$$E_c = \frac{1}{0.75 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$E_c = \frac{1}{0.375 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$E_c \approx 2.67 \times 10^6 \; \text{V/m}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kV/cm:**

$$E_c = 2.67 \times 10^6 \; \text{V/m} \times \frac{1 \; \text{kV}}{1000 \; \text{V}} \times \frac{1 \; \text{m}}{100 \; \text{cm}}$$

$$E_c \approx 26.7 \; \text{kV/cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **commercial PEF systems are designed at 25–35 kV/cm** — to ensure the applied field is comfortably above the critical threshold for gram-negative bacteria.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — NF vs RO vs PEF (for KTU Part B answers)

| Parameter | NF | RO | PEF |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Driving Force** | Hydraulic pressure | Hydraulic pressure | Electric field |
| **Energy Form** | Mechanical (pump) | Mechanical (HP pump) | Electrical (capacitor discharge) |
| **Mechanism** | Size + charge exclusion | Solution-Diffusion | Electroporation |
| **Thermal Damage?** | No | No (slight temp rise) | No (non-thermal) |
| **Microbial Inactivation?** | Limited | Limited | **Yes (5-log reduction typical)** |
| **Major Application** | Whey demineralization, water softening | Juice concentration, seawater desalination | Cold pasteurization of juices |
| **Operating Cost Driver** | Membrane replacement | High pressure pump electricity | Pulse generator + electrodes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any **three** key differences between Nanofiltration (NF) and Reverse Osmosis (RO) membranes based on operating pressure, pore size, and rejection characteristics.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | NF | RO |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pore size | **0.5 – 2 nm** | < 0.5 nm (dense) |
| 2 | Operating pressure | **5 – 20 bar** | **10 – 80 bar** |
| 3 | Ion rejection | Rejects multivalent ions (Ca²⁺, SO₄²⁻); passes monovalent ions partially | Rejects **all** ions (95 – 99.8% for NaCl) |

(1 mark each for correctly identifying differences with values.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the principle of **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)** processing. What is **electroporation**?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Principle (1 mark):** PEF is a non-thermal preservation method where short, high-voltage pulses (**20–40 kV/cm**, µs duration) are applied to food placed between two electrodes. The pulses inactivate microorganisms by disrupting their cell membranes.
- **Electroporation (1 mark):** The formation of permanent or temporary pores in the lipid bilayer of microbial cell membranes when the induced transmembrane potential exceeds the critical value (~**1 V**).
- **Outcome (1 mark):** Microbial inactivation due to leakage of intracellular contents, while sensory/nutritional quality of the food is preserved.

---

### 📝 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

---

#### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Solution-Diffusion model** for transport through RO membranes. Derive the expressions for **water flux** and **solute flux**, and explain the role of **osmotic pressure**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A RO system is used to concentrate **orange juice** from **10 °Brix** to **50 °Brix**. Calculate the approximate osmotic pressure of the feed solution at **25 °C** using the Van't Hoff approximation (assume sugars behave as a single non-dissociating solute with average molecular weight **180 g/mol**). State one assumption and one limitation of this approximation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Solution-Diffusion Model (7 marks)**

1. **Model Premise (1 mark):** The RO membrane is treated as a *dense, non-porous* polymer film. Permeation occurs via:
   - **Sorption** of solvent/solute at the upstream face,
   - **Molecular diffusion** through the polymer matrix,
   - **Desorption** at the downstream face.

2. **Water Flux Equation (3 marks):**

$$J_w = A_w \left( \Delta P - \Delta \pi \right)$$

Where:
- $J_w$ = volumetric water flux (m³/m²·s)
- $A_w$ = water permeability constant (m/s·Pa)
- $\Delta P$ = applied transmembrane hydraulic pressure
- $\Delta \pi$ = osmotic pressure difference across membrane

3. **Solute Flux Equation (2 marks):**

$$J_s = B_s \left( C_f - C_p \right)$$

Where $B_s$ = solute permeability constant (m/s); $C_f, C_p$ = feed and permeate concentrations.

4. **Role of Osmotic Pressure (1 mark):** $\Delta\pi$ acts as the **counter-driving force**. RO proceeds only when $\Delta P > \Delta \pi$.

---

**(b) Osmotic Pressure Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Estimate feed solute concentration:**

Approximate 10 °Brix ≈ **10 g sugar / 100 g solution** ≈ 100 g/kg (treating density ≈ 1 kg/L → 100 g/L).

$$C_f = \frac{100}{180} \approx 0.556 \; \text{mol/L} = 556 \; \text{mol/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply Van't Hoff equation (i = 1 for non-dissociating sugar):**

$$\pi_f = 1 \times 556 \times 8.314 \times 298$$

$$\pi_f = 556 \times 2477.572$$

$$\pi_f \approx 1.378 \times 10^6 \; \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\pi_f \approx 13.78 \; \text{bar}}$$

**Step 3 — Assumption (1 mark):** Sugar behaves as a **single ideal non-dissociating solute**; activity coefficient = 1.

**Step 4 — Limitation (1 mark):** At **50 °Brix**, sugar solutions are highly *non-ideal* due to strong solute-solute interactions. Actual osmotic pressure from Raoult's law would be significantly lower (~5–8 bar at 50 °Brix) than Van't Hoff predictions — so Van't Hoff *overestimates* $\pi$ in concentrated juice.

---

#### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the principle and working of **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)** processing. With a neat block diagram, explain the components of a PEF system. List **two advantages** and **two limitations** of PEF. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the industrial applications of **NF** and **RO** in the food processing industry. Briefly discuss why NF is preferred over RO for **partial desalination** of cheese brine. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) PEF Principle, System & Evaluation (7 marks)**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** Short high-voltage pulses (**20–40 kV/cm**, 1–100 µs) are applied to food between two electrodes. The induced **transmembrane potential** $V_m = 0.75 \cdot r \cdot E \cdot \cos\theta$ exceeds the critical ~1 V, causing **irreversible electroporation** of microbial membranes and cell death.

2. **PEF System Components (2 marks) — Block Diagram:**

`DC Power Supply → Capacitor Bank → High-Voltage Switch → Pulse Forming Network → Treatment Chamber (with electrodes) → Pasteurized Product Outlet`

3. **Two Advantages (2 marks):**
   - **Non-thermal** — preserves vitamins (e.g., vitamin C), flavor, color.
   - Short treatment time (<1 s) — minimal energy input.

4. **Two Limitations (1 mark):**
   - Ineffective for products with **low electrical conductivity** or **high fat content** (acts as insulator).
   - Limited **shelf-life** extension compared to thermal sterilization; only 2–5 log microbial reduction.

---

**(b) NF & RO Industrial Applications (7 marks)**

1. **Reverse Osmosis Applications (3 marks):**
   - **Seawater & brackish water desalination** for potable water.
   - **Concentration of fruit juices** (apple, orange, grape) without thermal damage → retains fresh aroma.
   - **Pre-concentration of milk** prior to evaporation → reduced energy cost in dairy plants.
   - **Wastewater reuse** in food processing (e.g., brewery, dairy effluent reclamation).

2. **Nanofiltration Applications (2 marks):**
   - **Demineralization of whey** → NF selectively removes **Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻** (partial) while retaining valuable **lactose and proteins**.
   - **Water softening** → removes Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ hardness ions.
   - **Decolorization** in sugar refining and beverage clarification.

3. **Why NF is preferred for partial desalination of cheese brine (2 marks):**
   - Cheese brine contains **high-value NaCl (~ 18–20% w/v)** but accumulates **Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺** from cheese curd → these ions cause **scale formation** and texture defects.
   - NF **selectively removes the divalent cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺)** through Donnan exclusion while **retaining NaCl**, achieving partial desalination without losing essential salt.
   - RO would reject *all* salts, including the valuable NaCl — economically unviable.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students LOSE marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Osmotic Pressure Confusion:** Many students forget that $\Delta \pi$ must be *subtracted* from $\Delta P$ in the flux equation. RO *needs* $P > \pi$, not just $P$ alone. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing NF with RO:** Students interchange MWCO values or pressure ranges. Memorize: NF = 200–1000 Da, 5–20 bar; RO = <200 Da, 10–80 bar. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 3. **Van't Hoff factor error:** Using $i = 1$ for NaCl instead of $i = 2$. (–1 mark)
> 4. **PEF definition missing "non-thermal":** Examiner specifically looks for the *non-thermal* aspect and *electroporation* keyword. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Unit conversion errors:** Mixing bar, atm, and Pa. Always express final answer in **bar** for KTU problems involving RO. (–0.5 to –1 mark)

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist for last-minute revision before KTU exams.**

- ✅ NF pore size: **0.5 – 2 nm**; MWCO: **200 – 1000 Da**; pressure: **5 – 20 bar**.
- ✅ RO is dense/non-porous; MWCO: **< 200 Da**; pressure: **10 – 80 bar**.
- ✅ Solution-Diffusion model governs RO/NF transport — solvent dissolves → diffuses → desorbs.
- ✅ Flux equation: $J_w = A_w (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$; **RO works only if $\Delta P > \Delta \pi$**.
- ✅ Rejection $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$.
- ✅ Van't Hoff: $\pi = iCRT$, with $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$.
- ✅ NF rejects **multivalent** ions preferentially; RO rejects *all* ions (95–99.8%).
- ✅ PEF is **non-thermal**; uses **20–40 kV/cm**, **µs pulses**, induces **electroporation**.
- ✅ Critical transmembrane potential $V_c \approx 1$ V; $E_c = V_c / (0.75 r)$.
- ✅ NF applications: whey demineralization, water softening, sugar refining.
- ✅ RO applications: juice concentration, milk pre-concentration, desalination.
- ✅ PEF applications: juice pasteurization, plant tissue pretreatment, mass-transfer enhancement.
- ✅ **NF preferred over RO for cheese brine partial desalination** → retains NaCl, removes only Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ (Donnan exclusion).
- ✅ Energy density of PEF: $W = n E^2 \tau \sigma_{\text{elec}}$.
- ✅ PEF key advantage: preserves heat-labile vitamins (e.g., vitamin C) and flavor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process and Application of PEF in Food Processing Sector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF FOOD PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process and Application of PEF in Food Processing Sector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) Processing

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) Processing** is a non-thermal food preservation technique in which **short-duration high-voltage pulses** (typically **20–80 kV/cm**) are applied to food placed between two electrodes in a treatment chamber. The electric field induces **electroporation** of microbial cell membranes, leading to irreversible loss of membrane integrity and subsequent microbial inactivation, while preserving the sensory, nutritional, and functional qualities of the food because the treatment duration is too brief to generate significant thermal load.

Formally, the process is governed by the relationship between the **applied electric field strength (E)**, **pulse width (τ)**, **number of pulses (n)**, and the **specific energy input (Wₛ)** delivered per unit mass of the food product.

**Standard Metrics used in KTU evaluation:**

- **Electric Field Strength (E):** Typically **20–80 kV/cm** (or **2–8 kV/mm**) for microbial inactivation.
- **Pulse Width (τ):** **1–10 microseconds (μs)** for most industrial applications.
- **Number of Pulses (n):** Usually **10–100 pulses** depending on product.
- **Treatment Time:** Total exposure **< 1 second**, ensuring non-thermal character.
- **Temperature Rise:** Limited to **below 45 °C** for true non-thermal PEF operation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Lightning in a Juice Box":**
>
> Imagine a fruit juice flowing through a small chamber. Inside the chamber, two metal plates face each other (like two parallel walls). When a switch is flipped for a fraction of a millionth of a second, a very strong but very brief electric field jumps across the juice. Microbes (bacteria, yeasts, moulds) floating in the juice have thin, fatty membranes — the lightning-like jolt **punches microscopic holes** in those membranes (called **electroporation**). The cells leak their insides, cannot maintain their metabolism, and die. Meanwhile, the juice molecules (water, sugars, vitamins, pigments) are too small and too robust to be damaged — so the juice tastes fresh, looks bright, and retains its vitamin C, even though the microbes are destroyed.
>
> In essence: **PEF is to microbes what a thunderclap is to a fragile soap bubble — destructive to the bubble, harmless to the surrounding air.**

## 1.3 Historical Background and Significance

| Aspect | Detail |
| --- | --- |
| Origin | First reported inactivation effect observed by **Doevenspeck (1960s)** in Germany. |
| Industrial Pioneer | **Krupp/Mauss** and **Thomson** developed early continuous PEF systems. |
| Major Commercial System | **PurePulse Technologies** (USA), **Genesis Juices**, and **Euro Source** (Europe). |
| Commercial Status | First industrial PEF pasteurization line installed at **Genesis Juices, Oregon (2005)**. |
| KTU Relevance | Recognized as a **clean-label**, **green**, and **energy-efficient** non-thermal alternative to thermal pasteurization. |

> [!TIP]
> **Why PEF Matters for KTU Examinations:**
> PEF is a flagship **emerging non-thermal technology** in the KTU 2024 syllabus, frequently asked in Module 2 because it directly bridges *food microbiology, electrical engineering, and process engineering* — making it an ideal integrative question topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Principle (The "Why" and "How")

The PEF process operates on a sequence of well-defined biophysical and electrochemical stages:

- **Step 1 — Generation of High-Voltage Pulse:**
  A high-voltage pulse generator (capacitor bank, Marx generator, or solid-state switch) discharges a stored electrical energy through the food medium held in the treatment chamber. The pulse can be **exponential decay**, **square wave**, **bipolar**, or **oscillatory** in shape.

- **Step 2 — Application of Electric Field:**
  The voltage (V) applied across two electrodes separated by distance (d) creates a uniform electric field. The field polarizes every microbial cell suspended in the medium.

- **Step 3 — Membrane Polarization and Pore Formation (Electroporation):**
  When the **transmembrane potential (ΔΨₘ)** exceeds a critical value of approximately **1 V (≈ 10 kT/e)**, the lipid bilayer becomes unstable. Hydrophilic pores form, increasing membrane conductivity dramatically.

- **Step 4 — Irreversible Electroporation:**
  If the field strength and pulse duration exceed the **critical electric field strength (E꜀)**, the pores do not reseal. The membrane becomes permanently permeable — ions, ATP, and cytoplasmic contents escape; the cell cannot maintain homeostasis and dies.

- **Step 5 — Product Recovery:**
  The food exits the chamber with negligible temperature increase (ΔT typically **< 5 °C** per pass) but with a significantly reduced microbial load (often **3–6 log₁₀ reduction**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reversible vs. Irreversible Electroporation:**
> - **Reversible (E < E꜀):** Used in *electroporation-assisted extraction* (e.g., sugar from sugar beet, juice yield from fruits). Cells recover and remain viable.
> - **Irreversible (E > E꜀):** Used in *microbial inactivation and food preservation*. Cells die permanently.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units | Remarks |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Electric Field Strength | $E$ | $E = \dfrac{V}{d}$ | $\text{kV/cm}$ | $V$ = voltage; $d$ = electrode gap |
| Treatment Time | $t_{t}$ | $t_{t} = n \cdot \tau$ | $\text{s}$ | $n$ = number of pulses; $\tau$ = pulse width |
| Specific Energy Input | $W_{s}$ | $W_{s} = \dfrac{V^{2} \cdot \tau \cdot n}{R \cdot m}$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | $R$ = resistance; $m$ = mass |
| Energy per Pulse | $W_{p}$ | $W_{p} = \dfrac{V^{2} \cdot \tau}{R}$ | $\text{J}$ | Per single pulse |
| Power Dissipated | $P$ | $P = V^{2}/R$ | $\text{W}$ | Instantaneous |
| Temperature Rise | $\Delta T$ | $\Delta T = \dfrac{W_{s}}{c_{p}}$ | $\text{K}$ | $c_{p}$ = specific heat (~3.9 kJ/kg·K for water) |
| Critical Trans-Membrane Potential | $\Delta\Psi_{m}$ | $\Delta\Psi_{m} \approx 1\,\text{V}$ | $\text{V}$ | Threshold for electroporation |
| Log Reduction | $\log(N_{0}/N)$ | $\text{Microbial inactivation}$ | $\log_{10}$ | Typical PEF: 3–6 logs |
| Bipolar Pulse Factor | — | Bipolar reduces electrolytic deposition by ~50% | — | Compared to monopolar |
| Ohmic Heating Limit | $E$ | $E_{\max}$ for non-thermal ~ **30 kV/cm** for liquids | — | Above this, temperature rises sharply |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical substitution note for KTU answer sheets:**
> Always write **E in kV/cm** (not kV/m). Examiner marks are frequently lost when students mix units. $1\,\text{kV/cm} = 100{,}000\,\text{V/m}$.

## 2.3 Process Variables Affecting PEF Inactivation

The microbiological efficacy of PEF depends on five primary engineering variables:

- **Electric Field Strength (E):** Higher $E$ → greater log reduction. Doubling E can increase inactivation by **2–5 log cycles**.
- **Pulse Width (τ):** Longer pulses deliver more energy per pulse but may cause dielectric breakdown of food above **40 μs**.
- **Number of Pulses (n):** Inactivation follows a first-order kinetic approximation: $\log(N) = \log(N_{0}) - k \cdot n$, where $k$ is the inactivation rate constant.
- **Pulse Polarity:** **Bipolar pulses** outperform monopolar by **30–60 %** in microbial kill and reduce electrode corrosion.
- **Pulse Waveform:** **Square-wave pulses** are most efficient because they maintain constant E throughout τ.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> The above formulas are used by **food process engineers** to:
> - **Design treatment chambers** (electrode gap, chamber geometry).
> - **Select pulse generators** (capacitor bank sizing, voltage rating).
> - **Estimate operating cost** (energy in kWh per litre of juice treated — typically **2–5 kWh/m³**, far less than thermal pasteurization at ~40 kWh/m³).
> - **Validate HACCP protocols** by demonstrating logarithmic microbial reduction.

## 2.4 Equipment Architecture — Components of a PEF System

| Component | Function | Typical Specification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| High-Voltage Pulse Generator | Produces pulses up to 50–80 kV | Marx generator / solid-state switches |
| Treatment Chamber | Houses food between electrodes | Coaxial, parallel plate, or co-field |
| Cooling System | Removes ohmic heat | Heat exchanger after chamber |
| Flow System | Continuous or batch pumping | Positive displacement pump |
| Control & Monitoring | Voltage, current, temperature | Oscilloscope, PLC, sensors |
| Power Supply | Charges capacitor banks | 10–400 kW industrial grade |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Electric Field Strength Between Parallel Electrodes

We begin with the fundamental relationship that defines the intensity of the electric field experienced by a microbial cell positioned between two parallel plate electrodes separated by a distance $d$ across which a potential difference $V$ is applied.

$$
E = \frac{V}{d}
$$

- $E$ = electric field strength (V/m or kV/cm)
- $V$ = applied voltage (V or kV)
- $d$ = electrode gap (m or cm)

**Conversion to common KTU units:**

$$
E\,\left[\text{kV/cm}\right] = \frac{V\,\left[\text{kV}\right]}{d\,\left[\text{cm}\right]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style):**
> A PEF chamber has an electrode gap of **0.3 cm**. The applied pulse voltage is **15 kV**. Calculate the electric field strength.
>
> **Step 1:** Identify given data. $V = 15\,\text{kV}$, $d = 0.3\,\text{cm}$.
>
> **Step 2:** Apply the formula.
>
> $$E = \frac{15\,\text{kV}}{0.3\,\text{cm}} = 50\,\text{kV/cm}$$
>
> **Step 3:** Convert to SI units for completeness.
>
> $$E = 50\,\text{kV/cm} = 50 \times 10^{3}\,\text{V} / 10^{-2}\,\text{m} = 5.0 \times 10^{6}\,\text{V/m}$$
>
> **Result:** $E = 5.0\,\text{MV/m}$, well within the lethal range for vegetative bacteria.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Energy Input (Wₛ)

The **specific energy input** quantifies the thermal-equivalent energy delivered per kilogram of food. It is derived from Ohm's law combined with the pulse parameters.

**Starting equation — Energy per pulse:**

$$
W_{p} = \frac{V^{2} \cdot \tau}{R}
$$

- $W_{p}$ = energy per pulse (J)
- $V$ = applied voltage (V)
- $\tau$ = pulse width (s)
- $R$ = electrical resistance of the food in the chamber (Ω)

**Total energy for $n$ pulses:**

$$
W_{total} = n \cdot W_{p} = \frac{V^{2} \cdot \tau \cdot n}{R}
$$

**Dividing by mass $m$ of food treated yields the specific energy input:**

$$
W_{s} = \frac{W_{total}}{m} = \frac{V^{2} \cdot \tau \cdot n}{R \cdot m}
$$

**Typical units and bounds:**

$$
W_{s} \in [50,\, 500]\,\text{kJ/kg} \quad \text{for industrial PEF pasteurization}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
A juice with $R = 50\,\Omega$ and $m = 1\,\text{kg}$ is treated at $V = 30\,\text{kV}$, $\tau = 2\,\mu\text{s}$, $n = 50$ pulses.

$$
W_{s} = \frac{(30{,}000)^{2} \cdot (2 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 50}{50 \cdot 1}
$$

$$
W_{s} = \frac{(9.0 \times 10^{8}) \cdot (1.0 \times 10^{-4})}{50}
$$

$$
W_{s} = \frac{9.0 \times 10^{4}}{50} = 1{,}800\,\text{J/kg} = 1.8\,\text{kJ/kg}
$$

This is a very low specific energy — confirming the **non-thermal nature** of PEF.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Temperature Rise from PEF Treatment

Because the energy delivered is mostly dissipated as heat (Joule heating), the temperature rise in the food is given by the energy balance:

$$
\Delta T = \frac{W_{s}}{c_{p}}
$$

- $c_{p}$ = specific heat capacity of the food (≈ **3.9 kJ/(kg·K)** for water-rich foods).

**Continuing the previous example:**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{1.8\,\text{kJ/kg}}{3.9\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}} \approx 0.46\,\text{K}
$$

This temperature rise of less than **0.5 °C** per pass confirms the **non-thermal** character — the product quality is essentially preserved.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Microbial Inactivation Kinetics

The PEF inactivation of vegetative microorganisms often follows **first-order kinetics**:

$$
\log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_{0}}\right) = -k \cdot n
$$

- $N_{0}$ = initial microbial count (CFU/mL)
- $N$ = surviving count after $n$ pulses
- $k$ = inactivation rate constant (log-reduction per pulse)

**Alternative representation using Hulsheger's model:**

$$
\log_{10}\left(\frac{N}{N_{0}}\right) = -b_{E}\left(E - E_{c}\right)
$$

- $E_{c}$ = critical electric field (≈ 8–12 kV/cm for *E. coli*)
- $b_{E}$ = regression coefficient (typical: 0.04–0.08 cm/kV)

> [!TIP]
> **Application in Industry:**
> Designers use this kinetic model to predict the **number of pulses needed** to achieve a target log-reduction (e.g., **5-log reduction** of *Listeria* in milk), which is the regulatory standard for pasteurized dairy products.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PEF vs. Other Preservation Technologies

| Criterion | **PEF** | **Thermal Pasteurization** | **High-Pressure Processing (HPP)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Operating Temperature | $<45\,°\text{C}$ | $72-95\,°\text{C}$ | $20-25\,°\text{C}$ (adiabatic) |
| Treatment Time | $<1\,\text{s}$ | $15\,\text{s}-30\,\text{min}$ | $3-7\,\text{min}$ |
| Log Reduction (vegetative) | $3-6$ | $5-7$ | $5-8$ |
| Nutrient Retention | Excellent | Moderate loss | Excellent |
| Sensory Quality | Fresh-like | Cooked notes | Fresh-like |
| Energy Consumption | **Low (2–5 kWh/m³)** | High (≈40 kWh/m³) | Moderate (10–15 kWh/m³) |
| Continuous Operation | **Yes** | Yes | No (batch) |
| Applicable to Solids | Limited | Yes | Yes (limited) |
| Capital Cost | Moderate-High | Low | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
